ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general steamed bun class (FOODON_03315595). No specific class exists for salted egg yolk custard bun; closest_parent match used as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers steamed bread/bun as the closest defensible category. No specific code exists for salted egg yolk custard bun filling variant.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for salted egg yolk custard bun. SNOMED CT does not have a specific food concept for this item.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical salted egg yolk custard bun weighs approximately 60 g and is commonly consumed as a single dim sum serving or breakfast item.

nutrient_source.title: Salted Egg Yolk Custard Bun (Liu Sha Bao) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Salted Egg Yolk Custard Bun (cr_id F05020105). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated at 6.5 g/100g based on known composition of butter and salted egg yolk as primary fat contributors, consistent with HPB data and USDA FoodData Central reference values for similar custard-filled steamed buns.

gi_source.title: Steamed wheat bun / Mantou — analogous GI estimate; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for salted egg yolk custard bun in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 72 is estimated from the closest analogous food: plain steamed wheat bun (mantou/baozi), which has a published GI of approximately 68–75 in the International Tables of Glycemic Index (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). The custard filling adds sugar (16 g/100g) and fat (13.78 g/100g), which may slightly moderate the GI relative to a plain bun; however, the high refined-flour base and high sugar content support a high GI classification. GL per serving calculated as: (72 × 44.5 × 0.60) / 100 = 19.2 g per 60 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (16.0 g/100g) with low dietary fibre (0.9 g/100g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention, raising blood pressure independently of the moderate sodium content (161 mg/100g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption to half a bun on rare occasions and avoid pairing with other high-sugar foods or beverages on the same day, as the high sugar load promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention even at moderate dietary sodium levels.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 16.0 g/100g exceeds the 15 g/100g threshold; carbohydrate 44.5 g/100g from refined wheat flour with only 0.9 g/100g dietary fibre produces a high glycemic index (~72) and glycemic load (~19 per serving), driving rapid postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this food to a very small portion on rare occasions; if consumed, pair with protein- and fibre-rich foods to partially attenuate the postprandial glucose response, and monitor blood glucose closely.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat of approximately 6.5 g/100g from butter and salted egg yolk, combined with high sugar (16.0 g/100g) and refined carbohydrate (44.5 g/100g), contributes to LDL cholesterol elevation and pro-atherogenic metabolic load.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat this as an occasional indulgence only, limiting to half a bun infrequently, as the combination of saturated fat (~6.5 g/100g) and high sugar promotes LDL elevation and endothelial inflammation relevant to atherosclerosis progression.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sugar 16.0 g/100g exceeds the 15 g/100g RA threshold for refined sugar promoting inflammatory mediators; estimated saturated fat ~6.5 g/100g from butter and salted egg yolk exceeds the 5 g/100g threshold, promoting TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokines.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this food during flares and limit it to rare occasions otherwise, as the combination of high refined sugar and saturated fat promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine activity relevant to RA disease activity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Salted Egg Yolk Custard Bun, known in Cantonese as Liu Sha Bao (流沙包), is a popular steamed dim sum bun originating from Cantonese cuisine and widely consumed in Singapore, Hong Kong, and across Southeast Asia. The bun consists of a soft, pillowy steamed wheat-flour dough encasing a rich, molten custard filling made from salted duck egg yolks, butter, sugar, milk powder, and custard powder. When freshly steamed and broken open, the filling flows out in a semi-liquid lava-like consistency, giving rise to its alternate names 'lava bun' and 'molten custard bun'. The flavour profile is a distinctive sweet-savoury combination: the saltiness of the cured egg yolk contrasts with the sweetness of the custard base, producing an umami-rich, buttery, and creamy taste. Nutritionally, each 100 g provides 330 kcal, 6.9 g protein, 13.78 g fat (estimated saturated fat ~6.5 g), and 44.5 g carbohydrate with 16.0 g sugar and only 0.9 g dietary fibre. The high sugar and refined carbohydrate content, combined with a high estimated glycemic index (~72), makes this food clinically significant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease. Sodium is moderate at 161 mg/100g, but the high sugar load drives insulin-mediated sodium retention relevant to blood pressure management. The food is not suitable for individuals with diabetes or those following low-sugar or low-saturated-fat dietary patterns. It is commonly served as a breakfast item or dim sum snack and is widely available at hawker centres, dim sum restaurants, and bakeries across Singapore.

Is flowing custard bun high in fat?
What is the difference between liu sha bao and regular char siu bao?
Is salted egg lava bun halal?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05020105) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from known ingredient composition (butter and salted egg yolk) consistent with USDA FoodData Central reference values for similar products. GI estimated from closest analogous food (steamed wheat bun/mantou) per published International Tables of Glycemic Index (Atkinson et al., 2008). All mandatory multilingual names present for Singaporean cuisine category.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (6.5 g/100g) is estimated from the known fat contributors in the custard filling (butter and salted egg yolk) and is not directly reported in the HPB source entry; this value is consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for similar custard-filled steamed buns. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on steamed wheat bun (mantou/baozi) from the International Tables of Glycemic Index (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283); no direct GI measurement for salted egg yolk custard bun was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. Halal status is marked false as salted egg yolk custard buns may contain lard or non-halal shortening in traditional preparations; halal-certified versions exist but cannot be assumed for a generic record. 5. All core macros (protein_g 6.9, fat_g 13.78, carbohydrate_g 44.5) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification is required.
